content.problemStatement:
Chef owns a swimming pool with width NNN meters which contains NNN continuous walls with height hih_ihi​ (possible zero) and width 111 meter each.

When it rains heavily, water is stored in the swimming pool in the form of sections (check Figure 1). Chef is busy calling his friends for a party. So, you need to determine the maximum water stored in **a section** among all the sections after a heavy rain.

Figure 1 :



This swimming pool has 3 sections :


Section 2 has the maximum water i.e 10 units.
Section 1 has 9 units of water.
Section 3 contains 2 units of water.
So, the answer would be 10 units (maximum of all the sections)


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, number of testcases. Then the testcases follow.
First line of each testcase contains an integer NNN - number of walls AAA.
Second line contains N space separated integers h1,h2,.....,hNh_1, h_2, ....., h_Nh1​,h2​,.....,hN​


Output Format
For each testcase, output one integer in a single line - Maximum water stored among all sections.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 100000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the number of walls. The second line contains N space‑separated integers h1 h2 … hN representing the heights of the walls.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the maximum amount of water stored in any contiguous section after the rain.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: heights [5,0,2,6] form one water section. The water level is min(5,6)=5, so water amounts are (5-0)+(5-2)=8.\nTest case 2: heights [3,1,4,0,3,5] split at the wall of height 4. Left section stores 2 units, right section stores 5 units, maximum is 5.
Water is trapped at positions 1 and 3 (0‑based) each with 2 units, but they are separated by a zero‑water position, so the largest contiguous section contains only 2 units.

solution.approach: For each position i we need the highest wall on its left (including i) and the highest wall on its right. The water that can stay at i is max(0, min(leftMax[i], rightMax[i]) - h[i]). After computing water[i] for all i we scan the array and sum consecutive positive water values – a zero water position breaks a section. The maximum sum among all such sections is the answer. The left and right maxima can be obtained in O(N) time with two passes, or with a two‑pointer technique that uses O(1) extra space. The overall complexity per test case is O(N) time and O(N) auxiliary space (or O(1) if the two‑pointer variant is used).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) auxiliary space (arrays leftMax, rightMax, water). Can be reduced to O(1) with two‑pointer method.
